title: SEGMENT 7 — The mFruits and why monomeric matters

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — The mFruits and why monomeric matters
So, picking up where we left off: by the early 2000s, mRFP1 existed — a monomeric red fluorescent protein, finally — but it was dim and didn't mature fully.
The Tsien lab decided to fix that, and what they did between roughly 2004 and 2008 is one of the great campaigns of directed protein evolution in modern biology.
The method is worth understanding.

They took mRFP1 as a starting point, made tens of thousands of random mutant versions of it, and then screened them — basically by looking at petri plates and picking the brightest, best-behaved colonies.
Round after round.
Mutate, screen, pick the winners, mutate the winners again, screen again.
Out of this came a family of monomeric fluorescent proteins spanning yellow, orange, red, and far-red.
The lab named them after fruits.
The mFruits.
Let me walk through the key members, because you'll see these names constantly in modern papers.
mCherry — the workhorse red.
mCherry emerged from the mFruits screens as a good balance of brightness, monomericity, photostability, and reasonable maturation speed.
It absorbs around 587 nanometers, emits around 610.
It's not the brightest red protein available, but it's well-behaved when fused to other proteins, and it matures in a sensible amount of time.
For more than a decade, mCherry has been the default red protein for routine fusion-protein work.
Pair it with EGFP and you have a clean two-color system: green and red, well-separated, both monomeric.
That combination — green and red, EGFP and mCherry — is probably the most common two-color setup in cell biology.
mOrange — exactly what it sounds like, emitting around 562 nanometers.
Useful when you want a color between green and red, often paired with cyan or blue for multi-color work.
tdTomato — this one's a special case worth explaining.
tdTomato is not a true monomer.
The "td" stands for "tandem dimer.
" The original Tomato protein was a dimer — two cans stuck together.

Rather than try to monomerize it, which would have cost a lot of brightness, the engineers did something clever: they genetically fused two copies of the protein head-to-tail into a single long polypeptide.
So the molecule you express comes off the ribosome as one continuous chain, but it folds up into what is functionally a covalently-linked dimer — two cans stuck together, but as one molecule.
Why bother?
Because tdTomato is extremely bright — among the brightest fluorescent proteins available.
And from the perspective of whatever protein you're fusing it to, it still behaves like a single tag.
The tradeoff is size: tdTomato is twice as big as a normal FP, which can be a problem for some applications.
But for things like labeling neurons where you want maximum signal in fine processes, tdTomato is hard to beat.
mScarlet — a more recent entry, from 2017, from a Dutch group.

mScarlet is a designed red fluorescent protein — its sequence was constructed with the help of structural principles rather than purely by random screening — and it is currently the brightest monomeric red fluorescent protein known.
If you're starting a new project today and you want a red tag, mScarlet or one of its successors is often the right starting point.
Now I want to step back and emphasize a principle, because it goes deeper than any individual protein.
The reason monomericity matters so much comes back to the analogy I introduced earlier: a good tag is invisible to the biology.
The tag is supposed to ride along passively.
The moment the tag starts pulling your protein into artificial clusters — into dimers or tetramers — the tag has become an actor in the play instead of a stage light.
It's affecting where your protein goes, how fast it diffuses, what it binds to.
The biology you measure is now contaminated by the tag's own behavior.
This is why, when you read a modern methods section and see "mCherry" or "mScarlet" or "mEGFP" with that lowercase "m" prefix, the m is telling you "monomeric.
" It's a flag that says: this version of the protein has been explicitly engineered not to self-associate.

Even EGFP, derived from wild-type GFP, has a tiny tendency to weakly dimerize at high concentrations, and there's a variant called mEGFP with a single mutation, A206K, that breaks that weak dimer interface.
For careful work, especially when you're tagging a membrane protein where local concentrations get high, you want the m version.
So when you choose a fluorescent tag for an experiment, the very first question after "what color?
" is "is it monomeric?
" If the answer is no, you may be measuring an artifact.
That's the lesson of the entire mFruits campaign: thousands of mutations, years of work, all in service of making sure the tag tells the truth instead of contaminating it.
